name: Kakuna
route: Kanto Route 24, Route 25, Viridian Forest, and Pattern Bush; Johto Ilex Forest, National Park, Route 2, Routes 30 and 31, Route 47, and headbutt-tree corridors; Central Kalos Santalune Forest; Hoenn Safari Zone records; Sinnoh Eterna Forest; real-world analogue: hanging pupae in mossy Olympic Peninsula forests, orchard windbreaks, and damp national park groves
habitat: Tree trunks, branch nooks, underside of large leaves, hollow bark seams, thorny thickets, damp forest edges, and protected nursery trees guarded by Beedrill patrols
behaviorTemperament: Nearly motionless, but not passive. Kakuna hardens when disturbed, heats noticeably during internal development, and may extend a poison barb if bitten or grabbed. Wild specimens rely on camouflage, shell toughness, and adult Beedrill response rather than escape.
diet: Does not normally feed in the field. Lives on stored larval reserves while reworking tissues into the adult body.
activityPattern: Stationary. Internal activity peaks during warm daylight and humid evenings. Shell hardening is most obvious when shaken, handled, or exposed to predator scent.
bestTimeToFind: Morning or late afternoon on shaded tree trunks, under large leaves, and in branch nooks near Weedle feeding damage
observationDifficulty: Hard. Its color and rigid posture resemble leaves, seed pods, or hardened sap masses. Colonies are easier to detect by Weedle feeding signs and Beedrill patrol loops.
conservationStatus: Protected in colony nurseries and old-growth forest corridors
populationStatus: Stable in protected forests, vulnerable in parks where people remove suspicious cocoons, spray insecticide, or clear hollow nesting trees
description: A Bug and Poison pupal Pokemon that appears almost immobile while its body reorganizes for evolution. Its hard shell, internal heat, and emergency barb make it dangerous to handle even before it becomes Beedrill.
lore: For centuries, Johto foresters called Kakuna 'hanging thorns' because the shells appeared overnight after Weedle feeding surges. Superstition said that if a child counted the cocoons on an Ilex tree, the number would double by morning and the forest would remember the child's voice. The truth is less magical and more dangerous. A mature Kakuna tree is never empty. Each shell is a living alarm bell, each warm casing a Beedrill not yet born. In old Viridian records, the worst swarm attacks began when collectors pried Kakuna from bark and carried them in sacks. The shells did not scream. They only clicked, warmed, and released scent. The adults did the screaming for them.
survivalUse: A Kakuna cluster indicates a nearby Weedle nursery and adult Beedrill patrol zone. Shed shell fragments can be studied for toxin research and lightweight chitin composites, but field collection should be avoided without permits.
handlingWarning: Do not pry Kakuna from bark, do not warm them artificially, and do not store them in sealed bags. Heat stress, vibration, and separation from colony scent can trigger emergency evolution or adult swarm response.
encounterAdvice: Observe from several yards away. Avoid standing directly under cocoon branches. If one falls, do not catch it bare-handed. Mark the location, retreat, and notify a ranger or licensed bug handler.
firstAid: Treat punctures like venom stings: wash, cool, immobilize the area, and monitor for allergic reaction. Shell abrasions can carry irritant dust, so rinse eyes or open cuts immediately.
companionSuitability: Poor as a companion; suitable only as a managed metamorphosis stage in licensed colony programs
fieldBehaviors: Clings to trees for long periods
Leans into bark grooves for camouflage
May undergo rapid emergency evolution under severe stress
signsOfPresence: Hard yellow shell shapes tucked under leaves
Warm casing when viewed with thermal optics
Clusters above stripped Weedle feeding zones
Fine silk anchor bands around branch forks
Beedrill patrol loops around specific trees
Faint clicking from bark during warm afternoons
equipment: Epinephrine auto-injector for known severe allergy
Permit tag and field marker tape
fieldNote: A warm Kakuna is not dying. It is building the adult. If the shell becomes hot and the Beedrill patrol noise stops, leave immediately.
originStory: Kakuna is the line's answer to the problem of becoming something more dangerous while still alive in a forest full of predators. Weedle reaches Level 7 after storing enough leaf protein, venom precursors, and silk-chitin material. Its outer tissues stiffen into a hanging shell while the internal body softens, rearranges, and grows compound eyes, wing bases, foreleg stingers, venom sacs, and flight muscles. The heat felt through the shell is the cost of that transformation. The stage is short but critical. Without Beedrill adults, most Kakuna would be eaten before emergence. With Beedrill adults, the whole tree becomes a defended nursery.

evolutionLine.name: Weedle
evolutionLine.note: Larval venom-feeder. Evolves when the body has stored enough leaf matter, venom protein, and silk-chitin material to seal itself into Kakuna.

evolutionLine.name: Kakuna
evolutionLine.note: Immobile pupal armor stage. Evolves once internal wing muscles, foreleg stingers, compound eyes, and venom reservoirs finish forming.

evolutionLine.name: Beedrill
evolutionLine.method: Final stage; Mega Evolution with Beedrillite
evolutionLine.note: Territorial flying colony defender. Capable of Mega Evolution into Mega Beedrill when bonded to a handler and exposed to Beedrillite.

mainLine.name: Weedle
mainLine.note: Small larval forest forager. Feeds on leaves, hides in grass and bushes, and relies on its head barb for defense.

mainLine.name: Kakuna
mainLine.note: Pupal armor stage. Nearly motionless outside, but hot and biologically active inside while preparing for final metamorphosis.

mainLine.name: Beedrill
mainLine.note: Final venomous flyer. Swarm defense, high-speed strikes, nest protection, and three-stinger combat define the adult form.

target.name: Mega Beedrill
target.note: Temporary high-speed combat form. Leg structures become stingers, wing count increases, and venom delivery becomes faster and more specialized.

humanConflictRisk: Medium near colony trees, Low if left undisturbed
domesticationStatus: Managed metamorphosis stage, not a normal companion stage
prey: Does not hunt. Vulnerable to birds, bats, small mammals, and parasitic insects while immobile.
territoryRange: Stationary on an anchor tree, usually inside a Beedrill patrol zone of 100 yards to 1 mile depending on colony size
lifeCycle: Evolves from Weedle starting at Level 7, anchors to bark or leaf cover, reorganizes internally, heats during metamorphosis, and evolves into Beedrill starting at Level 10.
containmentRequirements: Anchored branch section, stable humidity, airflow, low vibration, no sealed containers, sting-safe viewing distance, emergency Beedrill containment plan, and isolation from household pets
ecologicalRole: Stores larval biomass through metamorphosis, provides a defended prey source for specialist predators, and anchors Beedrill colony reproduction around stable trees.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Fungal shell rot, mites in silk anchors, failed shedding, heat stress, and venom-barb punctures are the main risks.
reproductionNesting: Kakuna itself does not reproduce. It is attached near larval feeding sites and future Beedrill patrol routes. Colonies often place many Kakuna on the same protected trees.
predators: Pidgey, Spearow, Fearow, Zubat, Noctowl, parasitic wasps, fungus, rodents, humans collecting illegally, and hungry bug-types
emergencyResponse: If a Kakuna is damaged, do not carry it through the forest. Back away, cover exposed skin, and leave the colony path. If shell cracking and intense buzzing begin together, expect adult emergence or swarm response.
legalStatus: Protected in many parks because removing Kakuna can collapse local Beedrill colony structure and cause swarm events
stressSicknessSigns: Cold shell during warm development periods
Failure to harden when touched with a probe
lifespan: Official lifespan is not stated. Field estimate: Kakuna stage lasts days to weeks before Beedrill emergence in healthy conditions; unevolved managed specimens may persist several months, but long-term suppression is unhealthy.
socialStructure: Stationary colony nursery stage. Kakuna communicate mostly through scent, vibration, and Beedrill response rather than visible behavior.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Defensive capacity is shell hardening and venom barb extension.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Barb contact range is short, but colony alarm scent can affect Beedrill behavior across the tree.
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent anchored stability if silk and bark remain intact

trainingRisk: Failed metamorphosis, premature Beedrill emergence, handler punctures, colony aggression, fungal shell rot, and stress death from rough transport
physiology: Kakuna is an armored pupal organism. The rigid outer casing protects a soft internal body that is rapidly forming adult Beedrill structures. Heat is a normal sign of intense internal activity, but excessive heat, odor, or leakage indicates danger.

keyFacts.diet: Does not normally feed during this stage
keyFacts.habitat: Tree trunks, branch nooks, leaves, and guarded forest nurseries
keyFacts.lifespan: Official not stated. Field estimate: days to weeks before evolution; several months if prevented from evolving under managed conditions.

domesticationDifficulty: High for normal owners. The animal appears easy because it barely moves, but the risks are hidden in temperature control, shell damage, and the adult form that follows.
physicalCharacteristics: Yellow segmented cocoon body with narrow dark eyes, rigid shell plates, pointed lower body, limited movement, hidden developing adult tissues, and a small poison barb used only under threat.
dietRestrictions: Do not feed unless directed by a specialist. Avoid sealed humid containers, dry heat lamps, insecticides, moldy bark, vibration, and forced shell cleaning.

attacks.name: Poison Sting
attacks.fieldUse: Emergency barb extension from the shell when handled or bitten.

attacks.name: String Shot
attacks.fieldUse: Residual silk discharge used around its anchor point.

attacks.name: Harden
attacks.fieldUse: Rapid shell tensioning that makes the outer casing harder and more difficult to crush.

attacks.name: Iron Defense
attacks.fieldUse: Advanced defensive training response that reinforces the shell beyond normal hardening.

sinnoh: Grand Underground caves in Shining Pearl after National Pokedex
unova: Route 12 through evolved forms or breeding
alola: Limited transfer or Route 4 for Beedrill in Ultra Moon
lumiose: Legends: Z-A Vert District, Bleu District, Magenta District, Rouge District, Jaune District, Wild Zone 1, and bug-type wild zones where available

officialEvolutionSummary: Weedle evolves into Kakuna starting at Level 7. Kakuna evolves into Beedrill starting at Level 10. Beedrill can Mega Evolve into Mega Beedrill using Beedrillite.
sourceUseNote: Core type, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, moves, and game-location anchors were checked against current online Pokemon database sources. Lifespan and real-world survival ecology are field-guide extrapolations because official lifespan is not stated.
